intro_text:
Playa del Médano sits on the southeast corner of Tenerife, in Granadilla de Abona, about 3 kilometres from Tenerife South airport. It is the windiest stretch of an already windy island, and the wind is the whole point. The northeast trade winds that blow across the open Atlantic get squeezed and accelerated as they round the south of the island, and they land here with a consistency tour operators put at roughly 300 days a year. The same wind that fills a kite makes the beach a poor place to lie still on a towel.

The main municipal beach, Playa de Leocadio Machado, runs about 750 metres long and 43 metres wide, fronted by a promenade and the low pastel buildings of the town. The sand is the dark, mineral kind you get on a volcanic island; you'll see it called both grey and golden online, which mostly reflects the light and how wet it is. The GloPrSM model classes it as lithic-rich, about 78% rock fragments — weathered off basalt, not washed in from a distant river.

The landmark at the south end is Montaña Roja, the Red Mountain: a 171-metre volcanic cone that anchors a 166-hectare Special Nature Reserve declared under Canary Islands Act 12/1994. The reserve protects an inorganic, sand-based ecosystem, with small saltwater ponds fed by seawater that filters up through the dunes at high tide. Sanderlings, whimbrels and ruddy turnstones work the tideline here.

El Médano started as a fishing settlement and grew slowly, which is why it reads differently from the package-resort strips west at Los Cristianos and Playa de las Américas. There's a substantial foreign-resident community, drawn first by the wind: as of 2013 around 41% of residents were of foreign origin, mainly Italian, German, British and Uruguayan. The result is a working town with a windsurf-shop economy, though that distinction gets harder to defend as new building creeps south toward the reserve.

The competitive centre of gravity is not Leocadio Machado but El Cabezo, the wave-and-wind beach just northeast of town, where the Tenerife stop of the PWA windsurfing world tour is sailed. Treat the municipal beach and the wider wind zone as two places sharing a town: one is where you walk the promenade, the other is where the world cup is decided.
spike_explainer:
El Médano's singular fact is meteorological, not scenic: it is one of the most reliably wind-blown beaches in Europe, and the entire identity of the place is built on top of that reliability rather than on the colour of the sand or the shape of the bay.

**Where the wind comes from.** The Canary Islands sit in the path of the northeast trade winds, the steady belt of air that flows around the eastern flank of the Azores High and pushes southwest across the Atlantic toward the equator. These are not gusts that come and go with weather fronts; they are a semi-permanent feature of the regional circulation, strongest in summer when the Azores High is at its most settled. The local beach data shows the seasonal signature plainly: mean wind speed peaks in June and July (around 5.8–5.9 m/s in the WorldClim record) and never collapses to calm even in the quieter autumn months. Tourism and windsurf sources commonly summarise this as roughly 300 windy days a year. That figure is a marketing round number rather than a measured constant, so treat it as the right order of magnitude, not a logged statistic.

**Why it concentrates here.** Tenerife is a high island. Mount Teide rises to 3,715 metres, and the bulk of the island forces the trade-wind flow to split and bend around it. As that air accelerates around the southern end and funnels through the gap between Tenerife and the neighbouring islands, the southeast coast around El Médano sits in the resulting acceleration zone. The effect is a venturi: the same volume of air squeezed through a narrower path moves faster. Montaña Roja and the local terrain add their own near-shore steering. The practical upshot is a beach that is windier, more often, and from a more dependable direction than almost anywhere a European windsurfer can drive or fly to cheaply in winter.

**The two spots that matter.** El Médano's wind zone is really two distinct sailing venues. Playa de Leocadio Machado, the town beach, is the flatter, more sheltered side and the place beginners and intermediates rig up. El Cabezo, the beach immediately northeast, is the wave spot: cross-onshore wind meets an Atlantic swell over a reef-and-sand bottom, which is what produces the jumping and wave-riding conditions a world tour needs. The local marine record shows wave heights averaging around 1.2 metres with the biggest months in late summer, and a typical period near 8–9 seconds — enough Atlantic groundswell to ride when the wind cooperates, without the size that would close the spot out.

**The competition history.** El Médano has hosted top-level windsurfing repeatedly. The Professional Windsurfers Association (PWA) world tour has run its Tenerife event here multiple times — sources describe the municipality as having staged a world-tour stop on the order of ten occasions — sailed at El Cabezo across wave and slalom disciplines, drawing on the order of 140 to 160 professional riders from around twenty countries. The 2024 edition returned after a roughly four-year gap, and the 2025 event was scheduled for 1–10 August. Exact field sizes and dates vary year to year and across promoter and federation sources, so the dependable claim is the pattern — a long-running, repeatedly held world-cup spot — rather than any single attendance number.

**Why the wind, and not the beach, is the spike.** Plenty of Atlantic beaches have dark volcanic sand and a photogenic headland. What El Médano has that they don't is a wind statistic dependable enough to build an industry on: rental centres, sail brands, instructor schools and a calendar of professional events that all assume the wind will show up. That dependability is also the honest cost. The thing that makes this a world-class board-sports venue makes it a frustrating place to do the ordinary beach things — sunbathe in stillness, swim in glassy water, eat lunch without sand in it. The wind is not a feature you visit despite; it is the entire reason the place exists in the form it does, and you either come to use it or you come knowing you'll be fighting it.

**Reading the conditions.** If you are choosing when to come, the trade-offs run opposite for different users. Board-sports visitors want the windy core of late spring through summer, when the trades are strongest and the world cup is sailed. Anyone hoping for a calmer beach day is better off in the autumn lull — the local record shows the lightest mean winds in November — and even then should expect breeze most afternoons. There is no still season here. That is the deal the geography offers, and El Médano has spent forty years turning it into the thing it is known for.
honest_reckoning_note:
The wind that made El Médano famous is the same wind that makes it a mediocre beach for most people. If you came to sunbathe, you will get sandblasted; if you came to swim in calm water, you will often be fighting chop and an onshore breeze. The water is the cool Atlantic, not the bathtub Mediterranean, and there is no monthly sea-surface temperature on record for this beach, so plan for cooler-than-it-looks swimming. The town is also genuinely busy and exposed, sitting right by the airport flight path, and the "authentic, non-package" character that sets it apart from Los Cristianos is being eroded in real time by new construction pushing south toward the Montaña Roja reserve.

The Blue Flag is worth a caveat too: Playa de Leocadio Machado held one from 2004 to 2016 and then stopped appearing on the list. That doesn't mean the water turned dangerous, but it does mean the beach lost a third-party quality certification it once advertised, and that is the honest version rather than the brochure one.
reckoning_pullquote: The wind is not a flaw you tolerate here; it is the entire reason the place exists, and it will ruin your sunbathing either way.

margin_notes.text: The sand is ~78% lithic fragments in the GloPrSM model — rock weathered off basalt, not river-sorted quartz. That's why it reads dark and feels coarser than a continental beach.
The 300-windy-days figure is a tourism round number, not a logged measurement. The defensible version: trade winds blow here far more often than they don't, peaking June–July.
Those saltwater ponds in the reserve aren't rain pools — they're seawater that filters up through the dune sand at high tide. Migrating shorebirds work the same tideline.
El Médano grew from a fishing settlement, which is why it feels unlike the built-to-order resorts west of here. The wind drew the foreign residents before the developers did.
El Cabezo, not the town beach, is where the world cup is sailed. Cross-onshore wind over a reef-and-sand bottom makes the jumping and wave-riding conditions a pro tour needs.
New building is creeping south toward the reserve boundary. The 'authentic, non-package' label that distinguishes El Médano is a present-tense argument, not a settled fact.

day_in_time.dawn: The wind is usually lightest at first light, so dawn is the one window for a calm-ish swim or a walk on Leocadio Machado before the rigs go up. Montaña Roja glows red-brown to the south as the sun clears the Atlantic horizon.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the trades are filling in and the first sails and kites are launching off El Cabezo. The promenade is busy; the sand is moving in the wind. This is working hours for the windsurf schools, not towel weather.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon is the strongest, most reliable wind of the day and the prime sailing slot — the water off El Cabezo crowds with riders. The low sun behind Montaña Roja is the classic El Médano photograph.
day_in_time.night: The wind usually eases after dark and the town's bars and seafront restaurants fill. The airport flight path is close enough that you'll hear and see late arrivals overhead.

food_drink.name: Pescado fresco (fresh local fish)
food_drink.description: Grilled day-boat fish — vieja (parrotfish) and cherne are the Canarian standards — typically served whole with papas and mojo. El Médano's fishing-village origin still shows up on menus.
food_drink.where: Seafront restaurants along the El Médano promenade

food_drink.name: Papas arrugadas con mojo
food_drink.description: Small Canarian potatoes boiled in heavily salted water until the skins wrinkle, served with mojo rojo (red, mild-to-spicy) and mojo verde (green, coriander/parsley). The default side everywhere on the island.

food_drink.name: Gofio
food_drink.description: Toasted, milled grain (wheat or maize) that is a Canarian staple going back to the Guanche period — stirred into soups, kneaded with stock, or used in desserts. A genuinely regional thing rather than a tourist invention.
food_drink.where: Canarian restaurants in Granadilla de Abona and El Médano

food_drink.name: Barraquito
food_drink.description: A layered Canarian coffee with condensed milk, liqueur (Licor 43), espresso, foamed milk, lemon peel and cinnamon — the local after-meal coffee.
food_drink.where: Cafés along the promenade and town square

timeline.title: Novel set in El Médano published
timeline.description: The novel 'El loco de la playa', set in El Médano, is published by La Prensa, an early literary appearance of the then-small settlement.

timeline.title: Montaña Roja declared a Special Nature Reserve
timeline.description: Canary Islands Autonomous Region Act 12/1994 classes the 166-hectare Montaña Roja area as a Special Nature Reserve and zone of environmental sensitivity, protecting its inorganic sand-based ecosystem and saltwater ponds.

timeline.title: Beach awarded European Blue Flag
timeline.description: Playa de Leocadio Machado receives the European Blue Flag, a water-quality and management certification it would hold for over a decade.

timeline.title: Foreign-resident share recorded at ~41%
timeline.description: Census-era figures put about 41% of El Médano's resident population as foreign-origin, mainly Italian, German, British and Uruguayan — a marker of the wind-driven international community.

timeline.title: Blue Flag lapses
timeline.description: Playa de Leocadio Machado last appears on the Blue Flag list in 2016, ending its 2004–2016 run of the certification.

timeline.title: PWA World Cup at El Médano
timeline.description: The Professional Windsurfers Association world tour stages its Tenerife event at El Cabezo, drawing top professional riders across wave and slalom disciplines.

timeline.title: Tenerife Windsurf World Cup returns
timeline.description: The PWA Tenerife event returns to El Médano after roughly four years away, sailed again at El Cabezo.

timeline.title: Grand Slam PWA World Cup scheduled
timeline.description: The PWA World Tour Tenerife El Médano stop is scheduled for 1–10 August 2025, billed with Wave and Slalom X disciplines and a field of professional windsurfers from around twenty nations.

zones.name: Playa de Leocadio Machado (town beach)
zones.character: The main municipal beach fronting the promenade; flatter, more sheltered water and the busiest stretch of sand.
zones.best_for: promenade walks, beginner/intermediate windsurf rig-up, town access
zones.notes: Held the Blue Flag 2004–2016; ~750 m long and ~43 m wide on average.

zones.name: El Cabezo
zones.character: The wave-and-wind beach northeast of town; cross-onshore wind over a reef-and-sand bottom.
zones.best_for: advanced wave windsurfing, the PWA world-cup venue
zones.notes: Technically the most demanding spot here; where the world tour is sailed.

zones.name: Montaña Roja / Leocadio Machado south end
zones.character: The wild south end under the red volcanic cone, inside the Special Nature Reserve; dunes, reddish sand and saltwater ponds.
zones.best_for: hiking the cone, shorebird watching, quieter (but still windy) sand
zones.notes: 166-hectare reserve; nudism is practised at this end. La Tejita beach lies just beyond the cone.

landmarks.name: Montaña Roja (Red Mountain)
landmarks.description: A 171-metre red volcanic cone anchoring a 166-hectare Special Nature Reserve (Act 12/1994). The summit trail is a moderate hike with panoramic coastal views; the surrounding reserve protects dunes, reddish sand and tidal saltwater ponds.

landmarks.name: Playa de Leocadio Machado promenade
landmarks.description: The pedestrian seafront that fronts the main town beach, lining the ~750-metre beach with cafés, windsurf shops and restaurants — the social spine of El Médano.

cultural_refs.title: El loco de la playa
cultural_refs.description: A novel set in El Médano, published by La Prensa — an early literary record of the settlement.

cultural_refs.title: Tenerife Windsurf World Cup (PWA Tenerife)
cultural_refs.creator: Professional Windsurfers Association
cultural_refs.description: The professional windsurfing world-tour stop repeatedly hosted at El Cabezo, the event most responsible for El Médano's international sporting reputation.

cultural_refs.title: Windsurf and kitesurf schools of El Médano
cultural_refs.description: A cluster of rental centres and instructor schools (windsurf, kitesurf, wing-foil) built around the dependable trade winds — the economic expression of the wind statistic.

recurring_events.name: PWA / Tenerife Windsurf World Cup
recurring_events.description: Professional windsurfing world-tour competition sailed at El Cabezo across wave and slalom disciplines, drawing roughly 140–160 pro riders from around twenty countries. Held repeatedly over the years; the 2025 edition was scheduled for 1–10 August.

things_to_know.label: Come for the wind or expect to fight it
things_to_know.note: There is no calm season. If you want a still beach day, target the autumn lull (lightest mean winds in November) and the dawn window, and still expect afternoon breeze.

things_to_know.label: Two beaches, one town
things_to_know.note: Leocadio Machado is the sheltered town beach; El Cabezo, northeast, is the demanding wave-sailing spot and world-cup venue. Pick the one that matches your level.

things_to_know.label: Reserve rules at the south end
things_to_know.note: Montaña Roja and its dunes are a protected Special Nature Reserve — stay on trails, don't disturb the saltwater ponds or shorebirds. Nudism is practised at the south end of the beach.

things_to_know.label: Cool Atlantic water, airport overhead
things_to_know.note: Swimming is the cooler Atlantic, not Mediterranean-warm, and there's no sea-surface temperature on record here. Tenerife South airport is ~3 km away, so expect aircraft noise.

access_note: El Médano is about 3 km from Tenerife South airport (TFS) and roughly 4 km from San Isidro. From the airport it's a short taxi or bus ride; the TF-1 motorway runs just inland, with the El Médano exit feeding into town. Buses (guaguas) connect to San Isidro, Los Cristianos and Santa Cruz. There is paid and street parking in town, but it fills on windy summer weekends and during the world cup, so arrive early or come by bus.
